posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Received this this morning - and the changes keep coming . . .

Dear Reader,

It gives me great pleasure to introduce to you the newest member of our reviewer team: Anthony Mueller. An accomplished sommelier and presenter based in Napa Valley, California, Anthony comes to us with more than 20 years of experience in the food and wine industry. He has worked as a sommelier in some of the top restaurants in the US, including the Michelin three-star The French Laundry in Yountville, California, Tribute in Michigan and Tarbell’s in Arizona. He holds numerous wine certifications, including the Advanced Sommelier certificate from the Court of Master Sommeliers, the Wine & Spirit Education Trust Level 3 Award in Wines, the Certified Specialist of Wine from the Society of Wine Educators and the Certified Sake Advisor certification from the Sake School of America. When he is not sparring with his friends in the sport of blind tasting, Mueller enjoys riding his motorcycle, exploring the beauty of Napa Valley. Although he has never been to culinary school, Mueller enjoys cooking elaborate meals in his small kitchen at home, preparing dishes made with ingredients grown from his home garden. Anthony will be taking over reviewer responsibility for the regions of Washington State and South Africa.

I would also like to take this opportunity to announce that Erin Brooks, our reviewer for Oregon, will be taking on more territory in the USA’s Pacific Northwest. From February 1st, Erin will be sharing the reporting of Sonoma County with me, focusing on the Pinot Noirs, Chardonnays and Zinfandels, while I will maintain responsibility for the Bordeaux varieties from this region. Erin will also take over the majority of the coverage of California Central Coast, while I will reassume some coverage of this vast area depending on grape/producer. And I will continue to cover all of Napa Valley and Bordeaux.

With the expansion of our reviewer team comes increased coverage of some of our most important regions and a few new regions to add to our world of wine reviews. William Kelley, who has already been doing an amazing job with stepping up our Burgundy coverage, has recently established a base for himself in Beaune and will be giving Burgundy even more love and attention beginning this year. He will also be taking over coverage of Champagne, English wines and Madeira. Meanwhile, Stephan Reinhardt, who had an overflowing plate of key regions, will be focusing even more of his attention now on coverage of Germany, Austria, Alsace, the Loire Valley and Switzerland.

The responsibilities of our other stellar reviewers—Joe Czerwinski, Luis Gutiérrez, Monica Larner, Mark Squires and Liwen Hao—remain unchanged. Full details of areas of responsibility and bios for our entire reviewer team are available to view here.

Warm wishes,

Lisa Perrotti-Brown, Editor-in-Chief

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked: Don’t see many changes to Wine Spectator. They must love working there.

posts.name: Brandon Redman
posts.cooked:
Interesting news about Anthony Mueller. I’m curious to see what a Napa Valley-based wine professional will think of Washington wines. New palates into the world of profession wine reviewing can’t be a bad thing, I suppose.

Update/edit: I googled him, and he would almost surely be a guy who I know I’ve seen post on (I think?) the Spectator forums under AZWineGuy.

posts.name: Morten Båtbukt
posts.cooked:
WTF is a “wine presenter?”

I assumed it was a synonym to sommelier. I present wines every day:”is this the wine you’ve ordered madam?”

WTF is a “wine presenter?”

I assumed it was a synonym to sommelier. I present wines every day:”is this the wine you’ve ordered madam?”

yeah, except she said “accomplished sommelier and presenter,” which suggests they aren’t the same thing.

I immediately thought about Vanna White or the women in hot pants at car shows

To be fair, I look awful in hot pants. Also, the role of a sommelier doesn’t seem so much to be the presenter of guest’s choices anymore, but what Noble Rot dubbed Obi Wine Kenobi: “this is not the wine you want…”

Also, in all seriousness I think it might be a fancy title for someone who holds tastings for an audience.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
WTF is a “wine presenter?”

Neal, you can call me a “wine presenter”. I sponsored and hosted a wine seminar once. It was quite the hit. The good thing was, after all the accolades and praise - I’m pretty sure everyone was crunk - I did not quit my day job. I only took the next day off, medical necessity.

Actually, there was also that time when MarcF surreptitiously videotaped me educating the boys on the glories of Colares and pre-phylloxera wines. They were riveted. I nailed it.

Robert “Wine Presenter” A. Jr.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
You guys crack me up about the SQN ratings, etc. This board freaks out if any of those wines gets below a 97, regardless of reviewer. I remember the uproar when the WS gave some of the wines low 90’s . . .

Yep, I do believe there are some ‘sacred’ producers who will continue to get some crazy high scores regardless of reviewer - begs the question of whether they are universally loved or if no one is willing to go ‘against the grain’.

Will Lisa handle folks like SQN and Saxum moving forward? That will be interesting to see indeed . . .

Cheers!

posts.name: Philip N. Jones
posts.cooked:
This subject made me curious whether the WA/Mark Squires bulletin board stll employs a filter to prevent anyone from using the word Beserkers on their board. So this morning I asked, without actually using the offensive word. Squires himself responded: stll filtered.
I have always been a fan of the WA, particularly when Parker was writing most of it. But less of a fan now. They never should have banned nonsubscribers; a poor business decision. And then when (as noted above) the new management casually commented (without thinking first) that wine reviewers are a dime a dozen (after one of their best quit). Not something they teach in business school. After all, the reviews (and the reviewers) are what they sell. And what we (I) buy. And pay for.
I still subscribe. But I wonder.
Phil Jones

posts.name: Randy Smith
posts.cooked: Larry, do scores still resonate with the buying public and move wine?

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked:
I think William Kelley’s writing on Burgundy is exceptional and worthy of a subscription to The Wine Advocate based solely on his work. His input on the board here is second to none.

Without ratting out anyone, I was told by one of TWA’s principal reviewers about 7 years ago (when RMP was still in charge), that people who reviewed wines for TWA were forbidden from posting here. I hope that rule has been rescinded because I agree with your assessment of Mr. Kelley’s posts. Since I see that he posted here yesterday, it looks promising.

You must not have been paying attention, as Mr. Kelley was hired by TWA over a year ago and has continued to be a valuable member of this community since he started there.

Larry, do scores still resonate with the buying public and move wine?

I won’t answer for Larry but I’ll answer from some experience - it depends on the score, the scorer, and the buyer. As I’ve posted before, the two scores that had distributors calling for the wine were the NYT the week it came out and the WA when it came out. The NYT effect was short-lived - it was whatever they wrote about that particular week. Didn’t really matter a month later. The WA had a lot of retailer subscribers, and I always thought their market was more in the business than to customers. They’d move expensive wine with RP scores. Others didn’t matter nearly as much. And if RP called something good and it was cheap, that could clear the shelves. People would come in for it specifically. It’s why retailers and the WA itself still talk about the RP Wine Advocate even though he apparently has little do with it these days.

With shelf talkers, if they’re going to move wine at all, it doesn’t really matter who the critic is. If people see the points by the wine and purchase accordingly, they usually don’t know one reviewer from another. That’s why there are dozens of critics popping up - put a big “92 points” next to a bottle and nobody who’s going to buy based on that cares where that 92 points came from.

These days I would suspect that social media and friends have as much influence as anyone. Suckling has a lot of followers but he does performances where he mingles with all the readers and since every wine gets 90 or more points, they are all convinced they’re drinking great stuff. And then they can post in Instagram or whatever and next year all their friends will want to come too.

People on this board crow about this or that wine getting high scores, but those folks are inclined to buy the wine anyway.

And notice that nobody talks about tasting notes moving wine. The irrelevance of those is captured beautifully in the Critic Bingo thread, where it’s obvious that TNs don’t have to make any sense whatsoever. It’s the score that matters.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked:
Larry, do scores still resonate with the buying public and move wine?

Absolutely, and epecially at the #1 retailer of wine in the US, Costco.

Really only Enthusiast, Advocate, Spectator, and Vinous in a broad retail sense, and then in smaller shops Burghound and Dunnuck, Wine & Spirits not so much.

Yes terrible decision. I will never give them a nickel. Personally I think we review wines better than they do. These 100 plus wine reviews in a day are not accurate anyway. Since I have been doing the 3 day wine approach I am really seeing the future of the younger wines I am trying. These pop and pour on $250 Napa wines are not necessarily accurate. Just my opinion.

posts.name: Herwig Janssen
posts.cooked: I fully agree with Jeremy : William’s contributions to the WA are exceptionally good . I also really like his postings on this board , they are always very valuable . ( he is not perfect , he scores too many 100 pointers )

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I think William Kelley’s writing on Burgundy is exceptional and worthy of a subscription to The Wine Advocate based solely on his work. His input on the board here is second to none.

+1

Thanks both, and to you, too, Herwig. The kind words are warmly appreciated. I hope you will like my Champagne coverage. I’ll be spending a lot of time there.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: William, admittedly Burgundy is not as divisive here as Bordeaux seems to be, but you have managed to do something unprecedented - get unanimous praise on WB for a TWA critic! Fully deserved too.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
William, admittedly Burgundy is not as divisive here as Bordeaux seems to be, but you have managed to do something unprecedented - get unanimous praise on WB for a TWA critic! Fully deserved too.

+1

If I want to know what a bunch of unknown wine critics think about a particular wine I’ll just go to Cellartracker… TWA is about as relevant to me now as Wine Spectator and Suckling. Kind of sad how steep and swift the decline has been. But I guess they can milk the name for a few more years.

I should add that I don’t mean to criticize the reviewers, who are undoubtedly working their asses off and may be great for all I know. But it takes time to decide whether a critic’s palate aligns with your own, and there has just been too much change at TWA for me to bother anymore.

TNs don’t have to make any sense whatsoever. It’s the score that matters.

Todd, I found your new signature.

This subject made me curious whether the WA/Mark Squires bulletin board stll employs a filter to prevent anyone from using the word Beserkers on their board. So this morning I asked, without actually using the offensive word. Squires himself responded: stll filtered.
I have always been a fan of the WA, particularly when Parker was writing most of it. But less of a fan now. They never should have banned nonsubscribers; a poor business decision. And then when (as noted above) the new management casually commented (without thinking first) that wine reviewers are a dime a dozen (after one of their best quit). Not something they teach in business school. After all, the reviews (and the reviewers) are what they sell. And what we (I) buy. And pay for.
I still subscribe. But I wonder.
Phil Jones

Yes terrible decision. I will never give them a nickel. Personally I think we review wines better than they do. These 100 plus wine reviews in a day are not accurate anyway. Since I have been doing the 3 day wine approach I am really seeing the future of the younger wines I am trying. These pop and pour on $250 Napa wines are not necessarily accurate. Just my opinion.

I’d put this a little differently. As I have expressed (too) often, crowdsourced wine reviews (and for that matter, reviews of nearly anything) are of zero value to me, so much of what is posted here in the form of tasting notes amounts to nothing but noise. The experienced palates who post frequently here, on the other hand, are an excellent guide for me. Similarly, the reviewers who work for Vinous or TWA become known quantities over time, and by definition, they are tasting wines for a living, so they inevitably have (or at least accumulate) experience. Their reviews almost always have some degree of credibility the average note here lacks.

So I don’t think that, for me, “we” do a “better” job than TWA as a whole does reviewing wines. I’d say it very much matters who is posting the note, here and there. Some are very revealing; most are not.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked: Neil good points. My issue with a lot of mass rating sessions is the wines get reputative. Any time I have done 10 Aussie Shiraz wines or 15 German Rieslings in a 2 hour tasting they all start tasting similar and I won’t get into Napa Valley over the top Cabs. I do see some major flaws in their ratings when they start giving out big scores for wines like the Prisoner or the Big Easy by Fess Parker. Big fruit and no acidity yet they get 95 points. I used to look at these ratings for buying purchases but now I look at Cellar Tracker users I trust.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked: Reading some comments on FB from a few Washington producers. They certainly were not happy with Wiliam’s coverage - anyone want to comment about this? I’m out of that loop . . .

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
This subject made me curious whether the WA/Mark Squires bulletin board stll employs a filter to prevent anyone from using the word Beserkers on their board. So this morning I asked, without actually using the offensive word. Squires himself responded: stll filtered.
I have always been a fan of the WA, particularly when Parker was writing most of it. But less of a fan now. They never should have banned nonsubscribers; a poor business decision. And then when (as noted above) the new management casually commented (without thinking first) that wine reviewers are a dime a dozen (after one of their best quit). Not something they teach in business school. After all, the reviews (and the reviewers) are what they sell. And what we (I) buy. And pay for.
I still subscribe. But I wonder.
Phil Jones

Yes terrible decision. I will never give them a nickel. Personally I think we review wines better than they do. These 100 plus wine reviews in a day are not accurate anyway. Since I have been doing the 3 day wine approach I am really seeing the future of the younger wines I am trying. These pop and pour on $250 Napa wines are not necessarily accurate. Just my opinion.

I’d put this a little differently. As I have expressed (too) often, crowdsourced wine reviews (and for that matter, reviews of nearly anything) are of zero value to me, so much of what is posted here in the form of tasting notes amounts to nothing but noise. The experienced palates who post frequently here, on the other hand, are an excellent guide for me. Similarly, the reviewers who work for Vinous or TWA become known quantities over time, and by definition, they are tasting wines for a living, so they inevitably have (or at least accumulate) experience. Their reviews almost always have some degree of credibility the average note here lacks.

So I don’t think that, for me, “we” do a “better” job than TWA as a whole does reviewing wines. I’d say it very much matters who is posting the note, here and there. Some are very revealing; most are not.

Always enjoyed your posts Neal.

I would say that I lean most heavily on the experienced palates here, and particularly in their area of expertise. People drink bottles, and follow the wines over time. That just makes a world of difference (IMO) in understanding leaner and more savory wines.

I do think that reviewers working for Vinous, WA, etc. become known quantities, and I respect the time and effort they take to build experience. I have tasted with or submitted to a pretty good range of reviewers and I do not have any credibility issues with any of them. I also have tremendous respect for the work they put in.

But the world of wine has gotten so broad that I have a hard time understanding how multiple regions can be covered adequately by any talent level of palate. I live and work in a relatively small wine producing region and can barely keep up with all that’s happening.

I enjoy Vinous and the Advocate but the majority of new producers I am buying come from reading here.

I think Neal is dead on. But people who pay that kind of attention are rare.

As someone said, the wines become “reputative”. Not sure if that was a typo or an attempt to coin a phrase, but I’ll take the latter. With the Wall Street buyers of yore, the reputation of the Bordeaux chateau was what mattered. Then it was the Parker score. Today on this board, it’s the “board darling”. But out in the real world, where most people have never spent any time on a wine board or reading wine journals with any regularity, the reputation of a reviewer doesn’t really matter because nobody knows one from another. It’s Yelp, or friends or Facebook associates. Get things “reputative” within a few small circles and you’re good. Get them reputative within a lot of circles and you have Meiomi!

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
I think William Kelley’s writing on Burgundy is exceptional and worthy of a subscription to The Wine Advocate based solely on his work. His input on the board here is second to none.

Without ratting out anyone, I was told by one of TWA’s principal reviewers about 7 years ago (when RMP was still in charge), that people who reviewed wines for TWA were forbidden from posting here. I hope that rule has been rescinded because I agree with your assessment of Mr. Kelley’s posts. Since I see that he posted here yesterday, it looks promising.

Mark Squires banned plenty of people, not just his own colleagues. The doomed and the banned weren’t a particularly exclusive club.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Larry, do scores still resonate with the buying public and move wine?

These days I would suspect that social media and friends have as much influence as anyone. Suckling has a lot of followers but he does performances where he mingles with all the readers and since every wine gets 90 or more points, they are all convinced they’re drinking great stuff. And then they can post in Instagram or whatever and next year all their friends will want to come too.

“Rev. Jones - I do declare your Kool Aid to be 98 pts!”

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
I think Neal is dead on. But people who pay that kind of attention are rare.

As someone said, the wines become “reputative”. Not sure if that was a typo or an attempt to coin a phrase, but I’ll take the latter. With the Wall Street buyers of yore, the reputation of the Bordeaux chateau was what mattered. Then it was the Parker score. Today on this board, it’s the “board darling”. But out in the real world, where most people have never spent any time on a wine board or reading wine journals with any regularity, the reputation of a reviewer doesn’t really matter because nobody knows one from another. It’s Yelp, or friends or Facebook associates. Get things “reputative” within a few small circles and you’re good. Get them reputative within a lot of circles and you have Meiomi!

Well played!
